18 min read
Just now

--

Owning the Harness · Local Models · The RL Gym — a practical blueprint for building unmetered, sovereign intelligence in your organisation

The Trifecta of Sovereign AI
On 12 June 2026, the U.S. Department of Commerce issued an export-control directive that suspended access to a frontier AI model family for nineteen days. Enterprises across the world discovered, overnight, that the intelligence powering their workflows could be switched off — not by a vendor outage, but by a government order. Four months earlier, the U.S. Department of War had designated a leading AI lab a “supply chain risk”. China, meanwhile, has ordered its state-funded data centres to stop buying foreign AI accelerators altogether.
The threat model has changed: it is no longer just the labs that control our access to intelligence — it is governments.
At the same time, the economics of renting intelligence have inverted. Per-token prices have fallen roughly 98% since 2022, yet enterprise AI bills have risen by an estimated 320% over the same period, because agentic workflows consume 5–30x more tokens per task than simple chat (Gartner, March 2026). Companies report blowing through full-year AI budgets by April. Metered intelligence, consumed at agentic scale, no longer makes financial sense as the only supply line.
This post proposes that organisations build the Trifecta of Sovereign AI — three mutually reinforcing capabilities that together deliver durable, unmetered, jurisdiction-proof intelligence:

• Own the harness. The scaffolding around a model — tools, context management, retries, evaluation — moves task success rates by 20–40 percentage points; swapping frontier models moves them by about 1 point. The harness is where the durable IP lives, and it is the layer nobody can export-control.

• Local models. The best open-weight models now trail the closed frontier by just 8 capability-index points — the same distance as two successive releases of one frontier family — a lag of roughly 4 months (Epoch AI, May 2026). On hard benchmarks the gap is measured in single-digit percentage points, not classes: DeepSeek-R1 landed within 2 percentage points of the closed frontier on MATH Level 5. Chosen well and run inside a strong harness, an open model can be every bit as good at your agentic tasks as a closed frontier model — because the harness moves agentic outcomes by 20–40 points while the model gap is a few. Connect your harnesses to open models on developers’ own machines and on privately deployed infrastructure.

• The RL gym. Fine-tuning and distillation let small open models beat frontier models on specific tasks — in one 2026 study, a tuned 27B model outperformed a frontier model by 60% on its specialist task at 50–80% lower cost. An RL gym built from your own workflows is how models evolve to your organisation’s standards, permanently.
The play: a three-phase programme — (1) harden and instrument your harnesses to be model-agnostic, (2) stand up a local-model evaluation lab (developer laptops + a private inference cluster) with a clear-eyed accuracy/hardware analysis, and (3) pilot an RL gym on two high-volume internal tasks. Each phase produces standalone value; together they compound into sovereignty.

1. The New Threat Model: Intelligence Is Now a Geopolitical Asset
For two years, enterprise AI strategy treated model access as a procurement question: pick a lab, negotiate a contract, manage the vendor. That framing is now dangerously incomplete. The actors who can deny us access to intelligence are no longer just the foundation labs — they are the governments those labs answer to.
1.1 The evidence, in one timeline

• January 2025 — DORA takes effect (EU). The Digital Operational Resilience Act treats AI providers as ICT third parties: financial entities must register them, quantify concentration risk, and demonstrate documented exit strategies. AI inference sits squarely inside the obligation.

• February 2026 — a lab is designated a national-security risk. The U.S. Department of War designated Anthropic a “supply chain risk to national security” after a contract dispute — the first time the label, previously reserved for firms tied to adversarial states, was applied to an American company.

• June 3, 2026 — the EU proposes CADA. The Cloud and AI Development Act defines four sovereignty assurance levels for public-sector AI, up to full EU ownership and supply-chain control — a template regulators in other jurisdictions are already studying.

• Ongoing — China ring-fences compute. Beijing has ordered state-funded data centres to stop purchasing foreign AI accelerators, and Chinese open-weight models are increasingly treated as strategic exports. Either bloc can, at any moment, decide its models are not available outside its borders.
JUNE 12, 2026 — THE NINETEEN-DAY SHUTDOWN. The U.S. Commerce Secretary issued an export-control directive suspending access to a frontier model family (Fable 5 / Mythos 5). For nineteen days, enterprises whose workflows depended on those endpoints had no recourse. Access was restored; certainty was not — the Biden-era AI Diffusion Rule has been rescinded and, as of July 2026, no replacement framework exists. This is the single clearest demonstration that model access is now a policy variable, not a procurement guarantee.
The market has already priced this in. The sovereign AI market was valued at $40 billion in 2025 and is projected to reach $148 billion by 2032 (20.6% CAGR). In a 2026 survey, 83% of companies said sovereign AI is at least moderately important to their strategy, and 77% now factor an AI vendor’s country of origin into selection. McKinsey finds most enterprises have sovereignty on the 2026 roadmap but few have an actionable plan — and that sovereign migrations take three to four years. The strategic conclusion is uncomfortable but simple: if you want optionality in 2029, the work starts now.
None of this argues for abandoning frontier APIs. It argues for the position every well-run treasury takes with any critical dependency: use the best supply while it is available, and own enough of the stack that no single supplier — or state — can hold your organisation’s productivity hostage. The rest of this post describes the three assets that create that position.

2. The Trifecta: Three Assets, One Flywheel
Sovereign AI is often reduced to “host a model on-prem.” That is one-third of the answer, and on its own the weakest third. A hosted model with no harness is a chatbot; a hosted model that never improves is a depreciating asset. Durable sovereignty requires three capabilities that reinforce each other:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Figure 1 — The trifecta as a flywheel. Each asset makes the other two more valuable; together they produce unmetered intelligence.The flywheel logic: the harness is where your developers and workflows actually live, and it is deliberately model-agnostic — so any model, cloud or local, can be plugged in behind it. Every task the harness executes produces traces: inputs, tool calls, outcomes, pass/fail signals. Those traces are exactly the raw material an RL gym needs to fine-tune and distil open models on organisation-specific work. The tuned models then run locally — on developer laptops or private clusters — and slot back into the same harness, at near-zero marginal cost. Each loop around the flywheel makes the next loop cheaper and better. Crucially, the flywheel spins in this order: harness first, local models second, RL gym third. Each stage de-risks and feeds the next.

3. Pillar One: Own the Harness
3.1 What a harness is — and why it is the real product
A harness (or scaffold) is everything wrapped around the raw model: how prompts are constructed, which tools the agent can call, how context and memory are managed, how errors are recovered, how outputs are validated, and how results are evaluated. Claude Code, Cursor and GitHub Copilot are, at their core, harnesses. The model is the engine; the harness is the car — and nobody commutes to work on a bare engine.
3.2 The data: the harness moves outcomes more than the model does
The strongest empirical finding in applied AI over the past year is that harness quality dominates model choice at the frontier:

• On SWE-bench Pro (real-world software engineering tasks), scaffold changes account for a 22+ point swing in success rate while swapping frontier models moves the score by roughly 1 point. Six frontier models now sit within 0.8 points of each other on SWE-bench Verified — the model has effectively commoditised at the top.

• Cursor’s agent-harness research showed the same model scoring 46% on one harness and 80% on another. Documented harness-only changes have swung the same model from 42% to 78% on coding benchmarks.

• Three different agent systems running the identical Claude Opus 4.5 weights on SWE-bench Pro produced scores from 50.2% to 55.4% — a 5.2-point spread from scaffolding alone.

• A 2026 consultancy case study: a team asked whether upgrading its model would fix a 34% task-completion rate. The harness was rebuilt instead — tool orchestration, error recovery, context compaction — and completion jumped to 71% with the model unchanged.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Figure 2 — Identical model weights, different harnesses. The scaffold, not the model, is the differentiator.The industry has a phrase for this: the model is the ceiling; the harness is the ladder. Frontier labs raise the ceiling a few points a quarter. A well-engineered harness determines whether you operate at 40% of that ceiling or 95% of it. And unlike the ceiling, the ladder is entirely yours to own.
3.3 Why owning it matters strategically

• It is the layer no one can deny you. Export controls apply to models and chips; they cannot apply to your own scaffolding, evals, tool integrations and workflow knowledge. When the June 2026 shutdown hit, organisations with model-agnostic harnesses re-pointed an endpoint; organisations wired directly to one vendor’s API stopped working.

• It encodes your organisation, not the vendor. Your compliance rules, coding standards, risk gates and data boundaries live in the harness. That is precisely the IP an enterprise should never rent.

• It converts model churn from risk into free upside. With six frontier models within a point of each other and open models four months behind, a harness that treats models as swappable commodities captures every price/performance improvement in the market — automatically.

• It is the prerequisite for pillars two and three. Local models are only useful if there is a harness to plug them into; an RL gym is only trainable if the harness is producing structured task traces and verifiable outcomes.
3.4 Actionable items — Pillar One

• Model-agnostic gateway: route every harness through a hybrid LLM gateway with per-task model routing, so cloud and local models are interchangeable behind one interface. Validate harnesses on cloud frontier models first; swap in self-hosted models as they qualify.

• Instrument everything: log task traces (prompts, tool calls, outcomes, human accept/reject) in a governed store from day one — this is the future RL-gym dataset.

• Build the evaluation harness as a first-class product: golden task suites per workflow (code review, migration, test generation, document drafting) with automated pass/fail. Evals are how you will later prove a local model is “good enough.”

4. Pillar Two: Local Models — On the Laptop and In the Enterprise
4.1 The industry has already voted: intelligence is moving on-device
This is not a contrarian bet; it is the direction the two largest developer ecosystems on Earth announced from their main stages:

• Microsoft Build: Foundry Local — announced at Build 2025 and generally available since April 2026 — runs open models entirely on-device across Windows, macOS and Linux with an OpenAI-compatible API, automatic NPU
